subject: Tort Law
lesson_title: The Duty of Care: Glencar Test, Special Duty Situations, Pure Economic Loss and Negligent Misstatement

questions.question: In Irish law, which is the authoritative test for establishing a duty of care in negligence?

options.A: The Glencar test — foreseeability, proximity, and no public policy reason to deny the duty
options.B: The Caparo test — foreseeability, proximity, and fair, just and reasonable
options.C: The Anns two-stage test — reasonable foreseeability plus absence of reasons to limit liability
options.D: The neighbour principle alone, as stated by Lord Atkin in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932]

questions.explanation: In Ireland, the authoritative test is the Glencar test, established by Keane CJ in Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84. It asks: (1) was damage of the relevant type reasonably foreseeable? (2) was there sufficient proximity? (3) is there any public policy reason to deny the duty? FE-1 candidates must not use the English Caparo test.

questions.question: What was the revolutionary significance of Lord Atkin's 'neighbour principle' in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932]?

options.A: It confirmed that a duty of care could only arise within recognised pre-existing categories of relationship
options.B: It established that a duty of care could arise outside any contractual relationship, based on reasonable foreseeability and proximity
options.C: It replaced the Glencar test in Ireland and adopted the English Caparo three-stage approach
options.D: It held that Mrs Donoghue was owed a duty of care under her contract with the café owner

questions.explanation: The revolutionary aspect of Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] was that Lord Atkin articulated a general principle — the neighbour principle — allowing duties of care to arise between strangers in the complete absence of any contractual relationship. Mrs Donoghue had no contract with the manufacturer, yet the House of Lords held that a duty arose by reason of foreseeability and proximity.

questions.question: In which case did the Irish Supreme Court establish a distinct Irish test for duty of care in negligence, separate from the English Caparo approach?

options.A: Donoghue v Stevenson [1932] AC 562
options.B: Caparo Industries plc v Dickman [1990] 2 AC 605
options.C: Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84
options.D: Ward v McMaster [1988] IR 337

questions.explanation: Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84 is the seminal Irish Supreme Court authority on duty of care. Keane CJ articulated the Irish three-limb test — foreseeability, proximity, and no policy reason to deny the duty — and expressly declined to adopt the English Caparo formulation.

questions.question: Under the Glencar test, what is the third limb that distinguishes it from the English Caparo test?

options.A: The plaintiff must positively establish that it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty of care
options.B: The defendant must have had a pre-existing relationship with the plaintiff before the act occurred
options.C: The damage must have been a direct result of the defendant's act rather than an omission
options.D: There must be no public policy reason to deny the existence of a duty of care

questions.explanation: The critical distinction: under Caparo, the plaintiff must affirmatively establish it is 'fair, just and reasonable' to impose a duty. Under Glencar, the court asks whether there is any public policy reason to deny the duty. The burden is different — under Glencar, the absence of a policy reason to deny the duty satisfies the third limb, rather than requiring positive proof of fairness.

questions.question: In which 2025 Irish Supreme Court case was it confirmed that the Glencar test applies in public authority cases, with particular attention to the policy limb?

options.A: Barlow v Minister for Communications [2025]
options.B: Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84
options.C: Ward v McMaster [1988] IR 337
options.D: Breslin v Corcoran [2003] 2 IR 203

questions.explanation: Barlow v Minister for Communications [2025] is the most recent Irish Supreme Court authority on duty of care. It affirmed that the Glencar test governs public authority cases and that, in such cases, the policy limb of the test must be examined with particular care, given the nature of public authority decision-making.

questions.question: What is the general rule in Irish tort law regarding omissions — failures to act?

options.A: There is always a duty to take positive steps to prevent harm to others who are in the defendant's vicinity
options.B: There is no general duty to act to prevent harm to another, even where action would be easy and effective
options.C: A duty to act arises automatically wherever the defendant was in a position to prevent the harm
options.D: A failure to act is treated as equivalent to a positive act for the purposes of negligence liability

questions.explanation: The general rule in Irish (and English) negligence law is that there is no duty to act — no duty to rescue a stranger in danger, even where intervention would be simple and costless. The rationale is that imposing a general positive duty to act would create an open-ended and unpredictable burden. Exceptions arise only in defined circumstances: assumed responsibility, special relationships, or creation of the dangerous situation.

questions.question: In Capital and Counties v Hampshire County Council [1997], on what basis was the fire brigade found liable?

options.A: The fire brigade had a general statutory duty to attend all fires reported within its area
options.B: The fire brigade had assumed a duty of care simply by attending the fire at the scene
options.C: The fire brigade had actively worsened the situation by switching off the building's sprinkler system
options.D: The fire brigade's failure to bring adequate equipment was an operational rather than a policy decision

questions.explanation: In Capital and Counties [1997], the English Court of Appeal held that a fire brigade does not generally owe a duty of care simply by attending a fire. However, in one of the joined cases, the brigade became liable because its officers had affirmatively worsened the situation by switching off the sprinkler system. This illustrates the distinction between pure omissions (no duty) and situations where an authority actively makes things worse.

questions.question: According to Breslin v Corcoran [2003], what is the general effect of a voluntary, independent criminal act of a third party on a negligence claim?

options.A: It increases the defendant's proportionate liability as a concurrent wrongdoer
options.B: It has no effect on the causal chain if the defendant's original negligent act created the opportunity
options.C: It converts the defendant's liability from sole to concurrent, reducing their contribution
options.D: It breaks the causal link between the defendant's negligence and the plaintiff's injury

questions.explanation: In Breslin v Corcoran [2003] 2 IR 203, the Irish Supreme Court confirmed that where a voluntary, independent criminal act of a third party intervenes between the defendant's negligence and the plaintiff's injury, that act will generally break the chain of causation (novus actus interveniens). The defendant's liability is extinguished because the third party's independent act is treated as the effective cause of the harm.

questions.question: In Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co [1970], on what basis was the Home Office held liable for damage caused by borstal boys who escaped?

options.A: The officers had a duty of control over the boys, and their negligent supervision made the escape and resulting damage foreseeable and proximate
options.B: The Home Office had a general statutory liability for all tortious acts committed by persons in its lawful custody
options.C: The boys' acts were treated as acts of the Home Office under the doctrine of vicarious liability
options.D: The Home Office had entered into a contractual duty to protect the yacht owners' property from interference

questions.explanation: In Home Office v Dorset Yacht Co [1970] AC 1004, the House of Lords held that the borstal officers owed a duty of care because they had assumed a position of control and supervision over the boys, and it was reasonably foreseeable that negligent supervision would create a risk of damage to property in the vicinity. The duty arose from the specific relationship of control, not from a general duty owed to the world.

questions.question: What is the general rule in Irish negligence law regarding liability for the independent wrongful acts of third parties?

options.A: A defendant is always liable for harm caused by a third party if they had any opportunity to prevent it
options.B: A defendant is generally not liable for the independent wrongful acts of a third party that cause harm to the plaintiff
options.C: A defendant is jointly and severally liable with any third party who causes harm in their immediate vicinity
options.D: Third-party liability is automatically imposed wherever there is a pre-existing relationship between defendant and plaintiff

questions.explanation: The general rule is that a defendant is not liable for the voluntary, independent acts of a third party. Exceptions arise where the defendant: (1) has assumed control over the third party; (2) is in a special position of control; or (3) has in some way facilitated the third party's wrongdoing. These exceptions are narrow and require clear justification.

questions.question: Under Irish law, when may a public authority owe a duty of care in negligence?

options.A: A public authority owes a duty of care in respect of all its activities, including discretionary decisions
options.B: A public authority is completely immune from negligence claims in the exercise of its statutory functions
options.C: A duty may arise in respect of operational decisions but not in respect of policy decisions involving the exercise of public discretion
options.D: A public authority's duty of care is governed exclusively by the English Caparo test, not by Glencar

questions.explanation: Irish courts distinguish between policy decisions (strategic, resource-allocation, or discretionary choices made in exercise of statutory power — no duty) and operational decisions (the manner in which a policy is implemented — a duty can arise). This distinction is applied under the Glencar framework, which requires the court to consider public policy when determining whether to impose a duty on a public authority.

questions.question: In Ward v McMaster [1988] IR 337, on what basis did the Irish Supreme Court find that a county council owed a duty of care to a purchaser?

options.A: The council had a statutory duty under the Housing Acts to ensure the structural safety of all residential properties
options.B: The council's inspection decision was an operational act, and operational acts always give rise to a duty of care
options.C: The plaintiff was in a direct contractual relationship with the council for the supply of housing services
options.D: The council had assumed responsibility by providing a valuation certificate on which the plaintiff foreseeably and reasonably relied

questions.explanation: Ward v McMaster [1988] IR 337 is a leading Irish authority on assumption of responsibility. The county council provided a valuation certificate in the context of a housing loan application. The Supreme Court held that by making this specific representation, knowing the purchaser would rely on it, the council assumed responsibility and thereby owed a duty of care. This is an example of the Hedley Byrne assumption of responsibility principle applied in an Irish public authority context.

questions.question: What does the 'foreseeability' limb of the Glencar test require?

options.A: That a reasonable person in the defendant's position would have foreseen the risk of damage of the type actually suffered by the plaintiff
options.B: That the defendant actually and subjectively foresaw the risk of harm before acting
options.C: That the plaintiff was known to the defendant personally at the time of the negligent act
options.D: That the specific type of harm suffered was identical to the risk the defendant had been formally warned about

questions.explanation: Foreseeability under Glencar is objective: would a reasonable person in the defendant's position have foreseen that the act or omission was likely to cause the type of damage the plaintiff suffered? The defendant's subjective awareness is irrelevant. The test focuses on the reasonable person, not the actual defendant's state of mind.

questions.question: What does the 'proximity' limb of the Glencar test require?

options.A: That the defendant and the plaintiff were in physical proximity at the time of the negligent act
options.B: That the parties were sufficiently close in their relationship that the law ought to impose a duty of care
options.C: That the plaintiff suffered harm within the defendant's property or area of control
options.D: That the defendant had personal knowledge of the plaintiff at the time of the alleged negligence

questions.explanation: Proximity in the Glencar test refers to the closeness of the relationship between the parties — not merely physical proximity. The court asks whether the relationship between the defendant and the plaintiff was of a character that made it appropriate for the law to impose a duty. This may involve reliance, vulnerability, control, or a pre-existing relationship — but not necessarily physical proximity.

questions.question: The general rule in Irish negligence law regarding pure economic loss is that:

options.A: Pure economic loss is always recoverable provided the defendant's negligence is clearly established
options.B: Pure economic loss is recoverable whenever it flows directly from the defendant's breach of duty
options.C: Pure economic loss — financial loss not consequential upon physical injury or property damage — is generally not recoverable in negligence
options.D: Pure economic loss is recoverable only where the defendant is a regulated professional giving advice in the course of their profession

questions.explanation: The courts are generally unwilling to impose liability for pure economic loss — that is, financial loss that is not consequential upon any physical injury to the person or property damage. The primary reason is the risk of indeterminate liability: a negligent act could foreseeably cause financial loss to an unlimited number of persons, creating crushing and unpredictable exposure.

questions.question: In Spartan Steel and Alloys v Martin [1973], which loss was the plaintiff unable to recover in negligence?

options.A: Damage to the physical metal melt that was in the furnace when the power was cut
options.B: Loss of profit on the specific melt that was physically damaged during the power outage
options.C: The cost of restarting and repairing the furnace after power was restored
options.D: Lost profits during the power cut attributable solely to the factory's inability to operate (pure economic loss)

questions.explanation: In Spartan Steel [1973] QB 27, the Court of Appeal held that the plaintiff could recover for physical damage to the melt in the furnace and for profits lost on that specific melt (consequential economic loss flowing from property damage). However, the plaintiff could NOT recover for profits lost across the entire factory during the power outage — this was pure economic loss, not flowing from any physical damage.

questions.question: Hedley Byrne and Co v Heller and Partners [1964] is significant because it established that:

options.A: A duty of care can arise in relation to negligent statements causing pure economic loss where there is a special relationship involving assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance
options.B: A bank can never be held liable for credit references it provides on behalf of its clients
options.C: A disclaimer of responsibility invariably provides a complete defence in any misstatement claim
options.D: Pure economic loss is always recoverable whenever the defendant is a financial institution or professional adviser

questions.explanation: Hedley Byrne and Co v Heller and Partners [1964] AC 465 is the foundational authority for liability for negligent misstatement. The House of Lords held that, but for the disclaimer, a duty of care would have arisen because there was a special relationship characterised by the defendant's assumption of responsibility and the plaintiff's reasonable reliance on that assumption. This carved out a major exception to the general rule against recovery for pure economic loss.

questions.question: In Hedley Byrne, why did the House of Lords ultimately find in favour of the defendant bank?

options.A: The bank had no special expertise in credit assessment and therefore owed no duty of care
options.B: The bank had expressly disclaimed responsibility for the credit reference, which negated any assumption of responsibility
options.C: The plaintiff had not suffered any actual financial loss caused by reliance on the credit reference
options.D: The relationship between the plaintiff and the bank lacked the proximity required to establish a duty

questions.explanation: The decisive factor in Hedley Byrne was the bank's express disclaimer of responsibility attached to the credit reference. The House of Lords held that this disclaimer negated the assumption of responsibility that is the necessary foundation of a Hedley Byrne duty of care. Without an assumption of responsibility, no special relationship existed, and therefore no duty of care arose.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a required element for liability in negligent misstatement under the Hedley Byrne principle?

options.A: The defendant possessed special skill or expertise relevant to the advice given
options.B: The plaintiff reasonably relied on the defendant's advice or information
options.C: The plaintiff and the defendant were in a pre-existing contractual relationship with each other
options.D: The defendant assumed responsibility for the accuracy of the advice, knowing the plaintiff would rely on it

questions.explanation: There is no requirement for a pre-existing contractual relationship under the Hedley Byrne principle. Indeed, one of the key contributions of the case was precisely that it recognised liability in the absence of contract. The required elements are: special skill or expertise; assumption of responsibility; reasonable reliance by the plaintiff; and resultant financial loss.

questions.question: In Wildgust v Bank of Ireland and Norwich Union [2006] IESC, on what basis did the Irish Supreme Court find a duty of care?

options.A: The bank had an express contractual obligation to provide correct advice on the insurance policy
options.B: The plaintiff had obtained a written warranty of accuracy from the defendant before relying on the advice
options.C: The defendant was the only available source of the relevant advice in the plaintiff's locality
options.D: The defendant had given incorrect advice about an insurance policy in circumstances where the plaintiff would foreseeably rely on that advice

questions.explanation: Wildgust v Bank of Ireland and Norwich Union [2006] is the leading Irish Supreme Court authority applying the Hedley Byrne principle. The defendant gave incorrect information about an insurance policy in a context where reliance was foreseeable. The Supreme Court held that a duty of care arose under the Hedley Byrne special relationship test — assumption of responsibility and reasonable reliance.

questions.question: Under the Glencar test, if both foreseeability and proximity are established, when will a court nonetheless refuse to impose a duty of care?

options.A: Where there is a sufficiently weighty public policy reason to deny the duty
options.B: Where the defendant acted in good faith and without dishonest intent at the time of the act
options.C: Where the damage resulted from an omission rather than a positive act by the defendant
options.D: Where the plaintiff failed to mitigate their loss at the earliest reasonable opportunity

questions.explanation: Even where foreseeability and proximity are both satisfied, the court will refuse to impose a duty under the Glencar test where there is a sufficient public policy reason to do so. Policy reasons include: the risk of indeterminate liability, the chilling effect on the exercise of public functions, the desirability of not over-deterring socially useful activities, and the availability of alternative means of redress.

questions.question: What is the primary rationale offered by Irish courts for their reluctance to impose liability for pure economic loss?

options.A: Pure economic loss is inherently unforeseeable and therefore always fails the foreseeability limb of the Glencar test
options.B: Imposing liability for pure economic loss risks creating indeterminate liability to an unforeseeable class of plaintiffs
options.C: Claims for pure economic loss are excluded by express provision of the Civil Liability Act 1961
options.D: Pure economic loss cannot be adequately quantified in monetary terms by Irish courts

questions.explanation: The courts' primary concern with pure economic loss is the 'floodgates' risk: a negligent act that causes financial loss — for example, a faulty report or a negligent inspection — could potentially expose the defendant to liability to an unlimited class of persons who read and rely on it. This risk of indeterminate liability to an indeterminate class justifies the general exclusionary rule.

questions.question: In which 2025 case did the Irish Supreme Court confirm that the Glencar test applies in public authority cases, with particular scrutiny of the policy limb?

options.A: Ward v McMaster [1988] IR 337
options.B: Breslin v Corcoran [2003] 2 IR 203
options.C: Barlow v Minister for Communications [2025]
options.D: Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002] 1 IR 84

questions.explanation: Barlow v Minister for Communications [2025] is the most recently decided Irish Supreme Court authority on the duty of care. It confirmed the continued application of the Glencar test and indicated that in public authority cases, the policy limb must be given particularly careful consideration, reflecting the special nature of state decision-making.

questions.question: Which of the following scenarios would most likely give rise to a duty to act as an exception to the general no-duty-to-rescue rule in Irish law?

options.A: A passerby who witnesses a stranger drowning in a pond but has no special training or relevant skill
options.B: A member of the public who could call emergency services at no personal risk or cost to themselves
options.C: A stranger who happens to be physically closest to the victim at the time the emergency arises
options.D: An employer whose employee is injured at work, given the special relationship of employment

questions.explanation: A duty to act arises in defined exceptional circumstances: assumption of responsibility for the plaintiff's safety, a special relationship (such as employer–employee), or the defendant having created the dangerous situation. The employer–employee relationship is a paradigm example of a special relationship that generates a positive duty to act. A random passerby, however near or capable, owes no general duty to rescue.

questions.question: Under Lord Atkin's neighbour principle in Donoghue v Stevenson [1932], who qualifies as a 'neighbour'?

options.A: Persons who are so closely and directly affected by the defendant's act that the defendant ought reasonably to have had them in contemplation when directing their mind to the act or omission
options.B: Persons who live or work in the immediate physical vicinity of the defendant's property or premises
options.C: Only persons with whom the defendant had a pre-existing legal, contractual, or family relationship
options.D: Persons who were physically present on the defendant's property at the time of the negligent act

questions.explanation: Lord Atkin's definition of 'neighbour' is the cornerstone of the duty of care. A neighbour is a person so closely and directly affected by the defendant's act that the defendant ought reasonably to have had them in contemplation when directing their mind to the acts or omissions which are called in question. This is broader than physical proximity — it encompasses any foreseeable victim of the defendant's conduct.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'assumption of responsibility' doctrine as applied in the Hedley Byrne line of cases?

options.A: Any professional who gives advice automatically assumes legal responsibility for its accuracy as a matter of law
options.B: A duty of care arises where the defendant, by their conduct, undertakes to exercise care in giving advice or information and the plaintiff reasonably relies on that undertaking
options.C: Assumption of responsibility can only be established by an express contractual warranty or written guarantee of accuracy
options.D: Assumption of responsibility arises only where the defendant has charged a fee for the advice given

questions.explanation: The assumption of responsibility doctrine does not require express words or a contract. It arises from the nature of the relationship and the circumstances — where the defendant, by their conduct, undertakes to exercise care and skill in giving advice or information, and the plaintiff reasonably relies on that undertaking. It is an objective assessment of whether the defendant voluntarily took responsibility.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT an established exception to the general rule that there is no duty to act in Irish negligence law?

options.A: The defendant has assumed responsibility for the plaintiff's safety
options.B: There is a special relationship between the parties, such as employer and employee
options.C: The defendant was present in a public place and was physically capable of providing assistance
options.D: The defendant created the dangerous situation that placed the plaintiff at risk of harm

questions.explanation: The mere fact that a defendant was physically present and capable of helping does not create a duty to act. The established exceptions are: (1) assumption of responsibility; (2) a special relationship; and (3) the defendant having created the dangerous situation. Physical presence and capability, without more, is insufficient to impose a positive duty to rescue under Irish law.

questions.question: In Spartan Steel [1973], which element of the plaintiff's loss was recoverable in negligence?

options.A: Profits lost across the entire factory during the period the power supply was interrupted
options.B: All costs incurred in restarting the factory after the power was restored
options.C: Wages paid to all employees who could not work during the power outage
options.D: Damage to the metal melt in the furnace at the time of the power cut and profits lost on that specific melt

questions.explanation: The Court of Appeal allowed the plaintiff to recover for: (1) the physical damage to the melt in the furnace (property damage); and (2) the profit lost on that specific melt as a direct consequence of the physical damage (consequential economic loss). Recovery was refused for all other profit losses during the outage, as those were pure economic loss not flowing from any property damage.

questions.question: Under the Glencar test, which of the following would a court primarily examine under the proximity limb?

options.A: Whether the nature of the relationship between the parties was such that it was appropriate for the law to impose a duty — for example, whether the defendant was in a position to control the risk of harm to the plaintiff
options.B: Whether the defendant and plaintiff were physically close to each other at the time of the negligent act or omission
options.C: Whether the harm occurred within Irish jurisdiction as opposed to a foreign jurisdiction
options.D: Whether the plaintiff formally notified the defendant of the specific risk of harm before the negligent act occurred

questions.explanation: Proximity under the Glencar test refers to the closeness of the legal relationship, not physical distance. The court considers whether the nature of the defendant's relationship with the plaintiff — in terms of control, reliance, assumption of responsibility, or direct dealings — makes it appropriate for the law to impose a duty. Physical proximity is relevant but not conclusive.

questions.question: The critical distinction between the Irish Glencar test and the English Caparo test in relation to the third limb is:

options.A: Under Glencar, the plaintiff must affirmatively prove it is fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty; under Caparo, the absence of policy reasons to deny a duty suffices
options.B: Under Glencar, the court asks whether there is any policy reason to deny the duty; under Caparo, the plaintiff must affirmatively establish it is fair, just and reasonable to impose the duty
options.C: Under Glencar, the third limb is simply foreseeability; under Caparo, it requires proximity in addition to foreseeability
options.D: The two tests are functionally identical and the distinction has no practical significance in Irish case law

questions.explanation: The key difference is the burden and framing of the third limb. Under Glencar: is there any policy reason to deny the duty? (the plaintiff does not need to positively prove fairness). Under Caparo: is it fair, just and reasonable to impose a duty? (the plaintiff must affirmatively satisfy this limb). In practice, Glencar is marginally more plaintiff-friendly on this third element.

questions.question: In the context of negligent misstatement, what is the significance of a disclaimer of responsibility?

options.A: A disclaimer has no legal effect once a special Hedley Byrne relationship has been established on the facts
options.B: A disclaimer automatically shifts liability to any third party who subsequently relied on the statement
options.C: A disclaimer may negate the assumption of responsibility that is the necessary foundation of a Hedley Byrne duty of care, thereby providing a complete defence
options.D: A disclaimer reduces the defendant's liability by 50% under the contributory negligence provisions of the Civil Liability Act 1961

questions.explanation: As illustrated in Hedley Byrne itself, an effective disclaimer of responsibility prevents the assumption of responsibility from arising, thereby precluding the duty of care that would otherwise exist. Without an assumption of responsibility, there is no special relationship, and therefore no duty. However, the disclaimer must be effective — courts have scrutinised disclaimers carefully, particularly where parties have unequal bargaining power.

questions.question: Which of the following best summarises the test established in Glencar Explorations plc v Mayo County Council [2002]?

options.A: A duty of care arises wherever the plaintiff suffered physical injury or damage to property caused by the defendant
options.B: A duty of care arises only where there is a pre-existing special relationship between the parties recognised by the courts
options.C: A duty of care arises where damage is foreseeable, there is sufficient proximity, and it is fair, just and reasonable to impose the duty
options.D: A duty of care arises where damage of the relevant type was reasonably foreseeable, there was sufficient proximity, and there is no public policy reason to deny the duty

questions.explanation: The Glencar test, as stated by Keane CJ, asks: (1) was it reasonably foreseeable that the plaintiff would suffer damage of the type claimed? (2) was there sufficient proximity between the parties? (3) is there any reason in public policy to deny the duty? This is the correct formulation — not the Caparo version which requires positive proof it is fair, just and reasonable. Option C states the Caparo test.
